Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and study for a few hours, but then I'd get distracted or just feel too anxious to focus properly. Most weeks I'd end up cramming things in whenever I could find the energy, which wasn't consistent at all. I probably spent around 22 hours a week on it, but those hours were scattered and not very productive. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to find videos or diagrams, but organizing when to actually study? That was always a struggle for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, honestly. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then anxiety would hit or I'd get caught up with my extracurricular stuff, and suddenly it's Thursday and I haven't done anything. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced me into action. Sometimes I'd study at 2 AM, other times mid-afternoon—there was no real consistency. I wish I could say I had a system, but I was just reacting to whatever felt most urgent at the time.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new assignment or announcement and that would prompt me to check in. Occasionally I'd log in just to see if there was anything I was forgetting about, which ironically made my anxiety worse. It wasn't like I had a habit of checking it daily—it was more reactive than proactive. The LMS felt like a reminder of all the things I wasn't doing right.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I tried to make it a habit early in the semester, but it never stuck. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I'd check in every other day, but then something would throw me off—stress, other commitments, just feeling overwhelmed—and I'd fall out of that pattern. The LMS never became automatic for me; it always felt like something I had to force myself to do. I think my anxiety actually made it harder to build that routine because logging in meant confronting all the stuff I was behind on.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I'd actually watch them pretty thoroughly—spent a lot of time on those. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing other students' perspectives helped things click sometimes. Anything with diagrams or visual explanations worked well for how my brain processes information. The written materials were harder for me to engage with, but videos I could actually sit through and absorb.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and a lot of the smaller assignments, which is probably why my assignment completion was so low. They felt overwhelming when I was already anxious, and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later. The discussion posts I participated in sometimes, but not as much as I should have. Anything that required a lot of sustained reading or writing felt like a mountain to climb when my anxiety was high. I'd minimize the tab and tell myself I'd come back to it, but often I didn't.
Q7: If I could see immediate relevance to an exam or a major assignment, it felt worth doing. Also, if something was visual and engaging rather than just walls of text, I was more likely to invest time in it. The CBT sessions I did actually helped me recognize when I was avoiding things, so sometimes I could push through. But honestly, if something felt like busywork or I couldn't see how it connected to my grade, my motivation tanked. I needed to feel like the effort would actually pay off.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist, basically. When I'm already struggling to keep up with required stuff, optional things just fall off my radar entirely. Required activities had this weight to them—like, I knew I had to do them eventually, even if I procrastinated. Optional ones were easy to justify skipping because there was no immediate consequence. It's not that I didn't think they'd be useful; I just didn't have the mental bandwidth to take on anything extra.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and review them offline because it felt less stressful than being on the LMS where I could see all my incomplete tasks. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations of concepts—sometimes other people explained things in ways that clicked better for me. I had notes in a physical notebook too that I'd review. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the computer entirely because screens started to stress me out after a while.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos on my tablet, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also think about course concepts while doing other things—like, I'd be at my extracurricular activities and suddenly connect something from class to real life. Sometimes I'd discuss course material with friends outside of the official discussion boards. The LMS only sees what happens on the LMS, but learning happens everywhere.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd watch a video once on the platform, download the slides, and then never go back to the LMS for that topic. I'd study from those downloaded materials for days sometimes. It was easier on my anxiety to work from local files than to keep logging back into a system that reminded me of everything I was behind on. So my actual engagement with the material was probably higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked really badly, and I just couldn't bring myself to face the coursework. I'd sit down to study and my mind would go blank, or I'd start catastrophizing about failing. There were also times when my extracurricular commitments got intense and I had to prioritize those. When stress and anxiety combined, the course became this thing I was avoiding rather than engaging with. The CBT sessions helped eventually, but there were some dark weeks in there.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. I'd log in, see all the tasks I hadn't completed, feel terrible about myself, and then close it without doing anything productive. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also messed with me—I'd waste energy trying to figure out priorities instead of just doing the work. My other responsibilities definitely competed for attention, and honestly, sometimes they won because they felt more manageable.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd have the lecture video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other things, zoning out, just not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. There were times I'd have materials open for an hour and retain almost nothing because my anxiety was so high. Being present physically isn't the same as being engaged mentally.
Q15: Constantly. I knew the assignments were important, but the anxiety made it feel impossible to start. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, or that I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better. The bigger the task seemed, the more I avoided it. It's like my brain was protecting me from the stress of doing it, but obviously that just made things worse in the long run. Even after CBT helped me recognize this pattern, breaking it was still really hard.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff for weeks and then panic-work right before it was due. The LMS became this place I'd frantically visit in the 24 hours before something was due, trying to figure out what I needed to submit. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could override my avoidance. Without deadlines, I honestly don't think I would have engaged at all some weeks.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. When it was positive or constructive, it motivated me a bit—like, okay, I'm not completely failing at this. But when feedback pointed out problems, it sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that area of the course even more. I didn't always check feedback promptly because I was scared of what it might say. The timing mattered too; if feedback came when I was already stressed, it didn't really register.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it didn't really account for people like me who struggle with consistency. Everything was laid out, which should have helped, but having all those modules and tasks visible at once was overwhelming. I would have done better with more guided pacing—like, "this week focus only on this." The flexibility that was supposed to be helpful actually gave me too much rope to hang myself with, if that makes sense. I needed more guardrails.
Q19: The video content being available was great for my visual learning style—that definitely made engagement easier. But the sheer number of small assignments and readings made it harder because I couldn't prioritize. Everything felt equally important and equally overwhelming. Clearer signposting of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped a lot. Also, the discussion board format felt a bit impersonal, which made it easy to disengage from.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. You might be logging in, watching videos, reading discussions—genuinely trying to learn—but then when it comes to actually submitting work, the anxiety kicks in. Or maybe the assignments feel disconnected from what you were engaging with. For me, consuming content felt safer than producing it. Submitting something means being judged, and that's terrifying when you're already anxious. High activity doesn't mean high output.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. I passed the course despite pretty low LMS engagement because I did a lot of studying offline and I'm decent at exams when I can cram effectively. The LMS doesn't capture everything—it doesn't see me reviewing downloaded materials, making notes, or discussing concepts with friends. Some people just learn differently and don't need to be constantly clicking around on a platform to absorb information. The system measures platform use, not learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. That's useful for identifying students who might be struggling to engage at all. But it completely misses the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental state behind the clicks. It can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. It also can't capture the anxiety or motivation driving someone's behavior. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when concepts stick with you outside of study time, when you want to understand rather than just pass. For me, real engagement happened in flashes—moments when something clicked and I got genuinely interested. But sustaining that engagement consistently was the challenge. It's mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels, which basically dictated everything about how I interacted with the course. It can't show the internal battle I fought every time I tried to make myself log in. It doesn't capture the offline studying, the conversations with friends about course material, or the moments of genuine understanding that happened away from the screen. It also can't show that despite my messy engagement patterns, I actually learned things and passed. The human experience behind the data is invisible.
